races.name: Itzulia Women — Stage 3 (Final)
races.finish_type: Uphill sprint / select group — after Mendizorrotz (6.4km @ 5.2%)
races.description: The decisive final stage of the 5th edition of Itzulia Women — the third leg of the 'Spanish trifecta' on the Women's WorldTour calendar (after La Vuelta Femenina and Navarra Women's Classic). Starting and finishing in Donostia (San Sebastián), the 113.1km stage is the hardest of the three days, featuring key Basque climbs borrowed from the men's Clásica San Sebastián: Jaizkibel (7.9km @ 5.6%), Gurutze, and the decisive Mendizorrotz (6.4km @ 5.2%), which crests with roughly 10km remaining and has historically been where the race is won or lost. Total climbing ~2,150m. Mischa Bredewold (SD Worx-Protime) leads the GC going into today: she won Stage 1 in Zarautz with a select-group sprint of five, then defended her yellow jersey on Stage 2 when Dominika Włodarczyk (UAE Team ADQ) took her first WorldTour victory in a closely fought sprint. Bredewold leads Yara Kastelijn (Fenix-Premier Tech) by 16 seconds and Riejanne Markus (Lidl-Trek) by 17 seconds — close enough that the stage outcome is fully open. Antonia Niedermaier (Canyon-SRAM) described herself as 'bullish' before today's stage. This is the exact same finale format that saw Demi Vollering win solo in 2025; without Vollering (who is preparing for the Giro d'Italia Women), Bredewold has her best chance yet of a first Itzulia overall.

gc_after_stage_2.notes: Niedrmaier, Berthet, Vallières also in contention within striking distance

stage_winners_so_far: S1 (Zarautz → Zarautz, 121.3km): Mischa Bredewold (SD Worx-Protime) — select sprint of 5
S2 (Abadiño → Amorebieta-Etxano, 138km): Dominika Włodarczyk (UAE Team ADQ) — first WorldTour win, narrow sprint
key_riders: Mischa Bredewold (SD Worx-Protime) — GC leader, two-time runner-up
Antonia Niedermaier (Canyon-SRAM zondacrypto) — 'bullish' ahead of mountain day
Juliette Berthet (FDJ United-SUEZ) — Vuelta Femenina podium, pure climber
Magdeleine Vallières (EF Education-Oatly) — World Champion, dangerous on climbs
Blanka Vas (SD Worx-Protime) — team support for Bredewold
Usoa Ostolaza (Laboral Kutxa Fundación Euskadi) — home rider, strong mountain legs

broadcasts.notes: Basque public broadcaster EITB provides free live coverage. Stream also available online via eitb.eus.
Eurosport carries the race across most European markets. Discovery+ for streaming access.
Sporza (Dutch) and Auvio (French) typically carry the decisive final climbs free.
Available via Eurosport on Discovery+. Check GCN+ for availability.

races.name: Giro d'Italia — Stage 9
races.finish_type: Mountaintop — Corno alle Scale Cat.1 (12.8km @ 5.9% avg; final 2.7km @ 10.1% avg, max 15%)
races.description:
The final stage before Rest Day 2 (Monday May 18) — and the last summit finish before the pivotal Stage 10 time trial (42km, Viareggio→Massa, Tuesday May 19). Stage 9 runs 184km from Cervia on the Adriatic coast into the Tuscan-Emilian Apennines. The profile is deceptively flat for the first 150km — including the 11.3km Cat-3 Querciola (4.3% avg) as a warm-up — before the stage explodes on the Cat-1 final climb to Corno alle Scale: 12.8km at 5.9% average, with the final 2.7km ramping to 10.1% average (max 15%). The summit sits at 1,200m altitude. This is a classic 'unipuerto' stage: one decisive climb, one opportunity to reshape the GC before the rest day reset. Last used as a Giro stage finish in 2004, when Gilberto Simoni won ahead of Damiano Cunego. The Red Bull KM bonus is placed 1.5km into the Corno alle Scale climb (6/4/2 sec bonuses), adding tactical spice for the GC riders.

Giro story so far (Stages 1–8): S1: Paul Magnier (Soudal-QS) wins sprint in Burgas, takes first Maglia Rosa. S2: Guillermo Thomas Silva (Bahrain-Victorious, URU) wins sprint in Veliko Tarnovo, takes GC lead. S3: Silva holds. S4 (Catanzaro→Cosenza): Jhonatan Narváez (UAE-XRG) wins; Giulio Ciccone (Lidl-Trek) takes Maglia Rosa via bonus seconds. S5 (Praia a Mare→Potenza): Igor Arrieta (UAE-XRG) wins; Afonso Eulálio (Bahrain-Victorious, POR) jumps 30 places into Maglia Rosa. S6 (Paestum→Naples): Davide Ballerini (XDS Astana) wins in crash-marred Naples sprint. S7 (Formia→Blockhaus, 244km): Jonas Vingegaard (Visma-LB) wins solo ahead of Felix Gall; Eulálio survives but loses time. S8 (Chieti→Fermo): Narváez solo wins his third stage, an Eulálio attack on the punchy climb is neutralised. UAE-XRG have lost 3 riders in stage 2 crash.

gc_after_stage_8.1st: Afonso Eulálio (Bahrain Victorious, POR) — Maglia Rosa 🩷
gc_after_stage_8.2nd: Jonas Vingegaard (Visma | Lease a Bike, DEN) — ~1:00 back
gc_after_stage_8.3rd: Felix Gall (Decathlon CMA CGM, AUT) — close to Vingegaard, 'super strong' on Blockhaus
gc_after_stage_8.notes: Thymen Arensman (INEOS) and Egan Bernal (INEOS) further back but still in race. Giulio Ciccone (Lidl-Trek) animated. Jan Christen (INEOS) leads Maglia Bianca. Narváez has 3 stage wins; Arrieta 1. Paul Magnier (Soudal-QS) leads Maglia Ciclamino (points).

stage_favourites: Jonas Vingegaard (Visma | Lease a Bike) — won Blockhaus solo, Corno alle Scale suits him
Felix Gall (Decathlon CMA CGM) — 'super strong', closest rival to Vingegaard on Blockhaus
Afonso Eulálio (Bahrain Victorious) — defending pink, needs to limit time to Vingegaard
Jan Christen (INEOS Grenadiers) — White jersey leader, aggressive puncheur-climber
Wout Poels (Unibet Rose Rockets) — solid form, needs breakaway
Einer Rubio (Movistar Team) — dangerous breakaway specialist
Filippo Zana (Soudal Quick-Step) — good climber, free role
Thymen Arensman (INEOS Grenadiers) — limited losses at Blockhaus
races.next_stage_note: Rest Day 2: Monday May 18. Stage 10: Tuesday May 19 — Viareggio → Massa, 42km individual time trial ⭐⭐⭐ — crucial for GC.

races.name: Tour de Hongrie — Stage 5 (Final)
races.finish_type: Punchy uphill finish — Szentkirályszabadja and Gella tackled multiple times
races.description: The final stage of the 47th Tour de Hongrie — a demanding 147.1km circuit through the Balaton Uplands finishing in Veszprém (European Capital of Sport 2026). Despite being the closing stage, it is far from straightforward: ~2,000m of elevation gain with multiple ascents of Szentkirályszabadja and Gella plus bonus sprint opportunities. The race started in Gyula–Békéscsaba (May 13) and has produced attacking racing across the week. Stage results: Tim Merlier (Soudal Quick-Step) won S1 (Békéscsaba flat sprint) and S3 (Kaposvár→Szekszárd sprint) — his continued return to form after injury. Benoît Cosnefroy (UAE-XRG) stunned the field on Stage 2 (Szarvas→Paks) with a long-range sprint on the steep Paks finish (final km @ 7%). Stage 4 (Mohács→Pécs, queen stage, 188km) was won by Jakob Söderqvist (Lidl-Trek) after the shortened, rain-soaked race over the Bárány Road / Lapis climb circuit in Pécs — who likely now leads the GC. Today's Veszprém finale offers the chance for late bonus-second battles to reshuffle the final standings before the podium in Veszprém, named European Capital of Sport for 2026.

gc_situation.leader: Jakob Söderqvist (Lidl-Trek) — likely leads after Stage 4 queen stage win
gc_situation.contenders: Benoît Cosnefroy (UAE-XRG), Luke Plapp (Jayco AlUla), Bauke Mollema (Lidl-Trek)
gc_situation.notes: Söderqvist won the Lapis/Bárány climb in Pécs on Stage 4 (chaotically shortened due to rain). GC standings TBC.

stage_winners_so_far: S1 (Gyula→Békéscsaba, 143.1km): Tim Merlier (Soudal Quick-Step) — flat sprint
S2 (Szarvas→Paks, 202km): Benoît Cosnefroy (UAE-XRG) — long-range sprint on Paks climb
S4 (Mohács→Pécs, 188km, queen): Jakob Söderqvist (Lidl-Trek) — rain-soaked, shortened
stage_favourites: Benoît Cosnefroy (UAE-XRG) — puncheur, explosive on short climbs
Luke Plapp (Team Jayco AlUla) — strong GC contender
Tim Merlier (Soudal Quick-Step) — two stage wins, may target bonus sprints
